cataloging_subject.label: Middle English Rimed Medical Treatise
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is Robert Max Garrett's complete article-level composite represented by CME00088: a seven-paragraph modern English preface, two distinct anonymous Middle English verse works transcribed continuously from British Library Add MS 17866, and Garrett's editorial dateline and signature. Garrett numbered the two poems as one because they are adjacent in the manuscript, but DIMEV records 4171 and 2343 and the rubricated shared boundary visible in Yale Takamiya MS 46 support separate work parts. This record is not one homogeneous medieval treatise, the complete Anglia volume, any one physical manuscript, the University of Michigan encoding, or the repository XML artifact.

catalog_summary.title: Middle English Rimed Medical Treatise
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous authors of two verse works
catalog_summary.date: Medieval composition dates unknown; surviving paired witnesses are dated from the later fourteenth century to ca. 1425, with conflicting individual witness dates; Garrett completed the article in 1909 and published it in 1911
catalog_summary.region: England; Add MS 17866 is scribally localized to Lincolnshire, while Takamiya MS 46 is described as East Anglian and by eLALME as southern Lincolnshire; composition place is unknown

notes: Garrett's article title is retained for the article-level composite; it is not presented as the original title of either medieval work.

titles.value: Middle English rimed medical treatise / [ed. Robert Max Garrett].

scope.description: Article-level selection and continuous numbering of the two manuscript works
Direct transcription of Add MS 17866, ff. 5r-21v
Comparison against Holthausen's printed X 90 edition

date_statements.display: The paired works are attested by ca. 1400-1425 in Takamiya MS 46; an older KB catalog dates X 90 to 1350-1399, while Holthausen allowed a fifteenth-century date

assertion.notes: The interval preserves conflicting witness dates and does not identify one confirmed earliest witness or provide an exact composition date.

date_statements.display: Garrett dated the completed article Seattle, 4 December 1909
Garrett's Add MS 17866 article and edition were published in 1911
notes: This is the first verified publication of Garrett's article-level composite, not the first publication of the X 90 versions, which Stephens printed in 1844.
A broad cultural and linguistic origin only; no exact place of composition is asserted.

scope.description: Scribal language of the Add MS 17866 versions

notes: DIMEV cites LALME/eLALME profile 1.100. This is not automatically the authorial dialect or composition place.

scope.description: Dialect traces in the Takamiya MS 46 versions

notes: Yale says the opening poems show traces of East Anglian dialect; DIMEV/eLALME instead describes the historical Takamiya witness language as southern Lincolnshire, leaving the precise relationship open.
Seattle is the place of Garrett's editorial completion, not a medieval place of composition.

scope.description: Rubrics, medical maxims, prayers, and formulae embedded in the Middle English works
A short French rue charm or formula followed by an English explanation

form_statements.description: Substantive modern English prose framing plus two Middle English verse works
Short rhyming couplets; the receipt collection is divided by rubric headings into 29 encoded line groups

genre_statements.label: Medical compilation
notes: A modern article-level composite of two independent medical verse works, not one organically unified medieval compilation.

subject_statements.label: Medical astrology and prognostication
notes: Includes instructions keyed to lunar and planetary conditions and tests of life, death, or hidden knowledge alongside herbal uses.

subject_statements.label: Christian healing charms and ritual
notes: Includes prayers and gathering rites in the herbal and a Saint Nicasius pox charm in the receipt collection.

content_parts.label: Garrett's comparative preface
content_parts.summary: Seven English prose paragraphs identify Add MS 17866, compare its two works and their order with Holthausen's X 90 edition, discuss the manuscript date and dialect, and list omissions and line correspondences.

content_parts.label: Of Herbs Twenty-Three
content_parts.summary: A 753-line anonymous Middle English herbal in rhyming couplets, opening with leek and then treating betony, centaury, marigold, celandine, pimpernel, motherwort, vervain, dragonwort, aristolochia, agrimony, mint, sage, rue, wormwood, fumitory, eyebright, and other plants through medicinal, protective, ritual, and astrological uses.
notes: The work ends and the receipt collection begins on the same Add MS 17866 f. 16r and Takamiya f. 14v surface.

content_parts.label: Collection of Medical Receipts in Couplets
content_parts.summary: A 370-line anonymous Middle English collection of rhymed remedies divided by 29 rubricated headings for headache, eye and ear disorders, loss of speech, wounds, gout, swellings, dropsy, bleeding, fistulas, cancer, pox, bone pain, felon, and related complaints.
notes: The first red rubric and incipit share the same physical surface as the end of the herbal in both directly mapped paired witnesses.

content_parts.label: Editorial dateline and signature
content_parts.summary: Garrett's University of Washington, Seattle dateline of 4 December 1909 and signature close the article.

summaries.value: Garrett's 1911 article presents two adjacent but independent anonymous Middle English medical poems from British Library Add MS 17866. A prose preface compares their order and readings with the Stockholm X 90 versions. The first poem is a 753-line herbal combining medicinal plant properties with prayers, charms, and astrological gathering instructions. The second is a 370-line series of rubricated remedies for ailments from headache and eye disease to wounds, dropsy, cancer, pox, and bone pain. Garrett numbers them continuously as one, but the manifest preserves the two-work boundary and includes his concluding 1909 dateline and signature as editorial framing.

CME00088 represents Garrett's complete printed pp. 163-193 article, including all modern framing and both medieval works. The XML has one preface DIV2 and one poem DIV2; the intellectual work boundary occurs inside the poem division between its first LG and the first LG TYPE='part', after line 753. Add MS 17866 f. 16r and Takamiya MS 46 f. 14v are shared boundary surfaces.

extent.description: Four cataloged content parts: a seven-paragraph prose preface, a 753-line herbal in one LG, a 370-line receipt collection in 29 rubricated LG elements, and one prose editorial closer. The complete XML has 31 PB and 256 MILESTONE elements. Counts describe this encoded representation, not a normalized critical edition of either work.

related_works.title: Macer Floridus / De viribus herbarum herbal tradition
notes: DIMEV says the texts grouped under record 4171 are based on Macer and vary substantially. This applies to the herbal part only and does not identify Garrett's or any medieval scribe's exact source exemplar.

evidence.citation: General Corpus CME00088 source XML and structural inspection.
evidence.locator: IDG; REVDESCR; TEXT/@LANG; DIV1; two DIV2; PB, MILESTONE, NOTE, P, LG, L, and CLOSER
evidence.summary: Verifies identifiers, complete article boundary, title, seven-paragraph preface, 753-line herbal, 370-line and 29-group receipt collection, shared line-753/754 boundary, page and folio milestones, editorial typography, and closing dateline.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, CME00088 and Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse collection.
evidence.summary: Confirms stable item identity, Garrett credit, 1911 source citation, public access, and collection context.

evidence.citation: Robert Max Garrett, 'Middle English Rimed Medical Treatise,' Anglia 34 (1911), 163-193.
evidence.summary: Direct evidence for article title and boundary, Garrett's responsibility and dateline, Add MS 17866 source and folios, two-as-one numbering, content and form, comparison with X 90 through Holthausen, and transcription typography.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Add MS 17866.
evidence.summary: Confirms current shelfmark, medicinal and herbal contents, vellum support, broad fourteenth-century date, and the absence of linked digitized content.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse, version 1.0, records 4171 and 2343.
evidence.locator: Records.xml records 4171 and 2343; Manuscripts.xml and MSSIndex.xml entries BLAdd17866, StockhX90, and TokyoTak46
evidence.summary: Supports two distinct work identities, IMEV numbers, title and incipit forms, subjects, couplet form, Add MS 17866 work loci and boundary, scribal localization, wider witnesses, and the historical Takamiya and X 90 records.

evidence.citation: Yale Library Digital Collections, 'Herbal in prose and verse,' Takamiya MS 46, object 16156709.
evidence.summary: Confirms ca. 1400-1425 English production, parchment, one hand, 43 leaves, Middle English and Latin, herbal and medical contents, East Anglian dialect traces, complete digitization, and current Yale identity.

evidence.citation: Direct visual inspection of Takamiya MS 46 ff. 3r, 14v, and 20v.
evidence.summary: Verifies the herbal opening, shared rubricated f. 14v boundary, receipt incipit, final receipt surface, red rubrics, and exact work-to-canvas mapping.

evidence.citation: Kungliga biblioteket nominal catalog card 21655, X 90, supplemented by the CME00099 X 90 review.
evidence.summary: Supports X 90 identity, older second-half-fourteenth-century date, microfilm negative, and the need to retain Holthausen's competing possible-fifteenth-century caveat.

evidence.citation: Middle English Dictionary citation '?c1450 Iff a man (Add 17866).'.
evidence.summary: Supports the MED's qualified Add MS 17866 citation date without displacing the separately attributed British Library or Garrett dates.
Provides entity, relation, source-edition, witness, complete-object cache, access, rights, editorial-practice, and unresolved-question evidence for this descriptive record.

notes: Garrett's singular article title and continuous lineation are edition-level choices; DIMEV's two work records and the shared rubricated manuscript boundary control the intellectual part model.
The prose preface and editorial closer are retained because CME00088 encodes the complete article, but their English language and prose form are not attributed to either medieval author.
Add MS 17866's Lincolnshire scribal localization and Takamiya MS 46's East Anglian or southern-Lincolnshire descriptions remain witness-level claims; no place of composition is inferred.

The complete Takamiya manuscript is locally cached and mapped at exact folio and canvas ranges. X 90 and Add MS 17866 remain request routes rather than falsely labeled undigitized or unavailable objects.

open_questions.question: When and where were the two verse works composed, and what source or compilation stages produced their divergent witness versions?
next_steps: Review current critical scholarship on DIMEV 4171 and 2343 and the Macer tradition.
Separate authorial dialect evidence from Add MS 17866, X 90, and Takamiya scribal language.
Do not assign one witness's production date or locality to the article-level composite or both abstract works.

open_questions.question: How should Add MS 17866 be dated and described in current specialist cataloging, and can its complete physical object be photographed?
next_steps: Ask the British Library to reconcile its fourteenth-century date with Garrett and the MED.
Request complete color photography and a modern foliation and collation statement, identifying ff. 5r-21v separately.
Retain the exact supplied-image license before any Git or publication use.

open_questions.question: Why does DIMEV record 2343 treat the whole Takamiya ff. 3r-20v paired sequence as one witness while record 4171 does not separately list its visibly bounded herbal?
next_steps: Ask DIMEV whether this is a deliberate witness-unit convention or a record-link omission.
Retain the direct f. 14v rubric evidence and do not collapse the two intellectual works while the census structure is reviewed.

open_questions.question: What item-specific terms govern redistribution and print-on-demand use of Yale's public Takamiya images and any future British Library or KB facsimiles?
next_steps: Keep all image caches Git-ignored and link the public Yale object while seeking written clarification for Git and commercial or noncommercial print-on-demand reuse.
Record KB's pending response and any British Library delivery terms at the exact access layer.
Keep underlying medieval text status separate from modern image and provider terms.
